protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority one breathing problem at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, my name is Alex and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I am having trouble breathing and my chest hurts really bad.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
My name is Kayla.
And can you tell me today's date and where you are?
I think it is June 28, 2019, and I'm on the second floor of my apartment.
You have a patent airway, Ma'am, so we can safely move forward with the evaluation.
Her respirations are very rapid and shallow, about 48 per minute.
Pulse is strong and regular. Her skin is warm and dry.
We'll do a more detailed assessment soon. First, can you tell me more about when your symptoms started?
It all started this morning when I woke up and was watching some TV. The pain and difficulty breathing started intermittently at first but got worse.
Did anyone try to help you before we arrived?
Yes, Kayla used an inhaler six times but had no improvement. That's when I called 911.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you describe the pain in your chest?
It is a sharp pain, and I'd say it's a 10 out of 10.
And does the pain go anywhere else when you breathe?
It's only in my chest.
Ma'am, are you experiencing any nasal congestion or sore throat?
No, nothing like that.
Respirations are 48 per minute and shallow.
Pulse is 105 and regular.
SpO2 is 75% on room air.
Blood pressure is 134 over 81 mmHg.
ETCO2 is 35 with a sharkfin waveform noted.
Blood glucose is 104.
EKG is in sinus rhythm.
We are going to monitor you with a 4-lead EKG and administer a treatment. First, we'll use a nebulizer to help open your airways.
Nebulizer is in place with 5mg albuterol and 0.5mg atrovent at 8 LPM. We'll proceed with IV access next.
We are also starting an IV on your arm to deliver more treatment.
Her respirations have slowed a bit, now around 18 per minute.
Pulse is down to 94 and remains regular.
SpO2 is now 97 with improvement.
Blood pressure is 127 over 77 now.
We are now administering 10 mg of dexamethasone intravenously to help reduce airway inflammation.
20g IV has been placed in your left arm and flushed with saline. Medication is being administered now.
A 4-lead EKG is in place for continuous monitoring. It's still in sinus rhythm.
Let me reassess your breathing. Do you have any known lung conditions or allergies?
I have moderate asthma and take Ventolin, Albuterol, and Singulair regularly.
Thank you. I'm going to listen to your lungs now. I hear wheezing bilaterally in all lung fields.
Your skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
She is alert and oriented to time and person.
Her GCS is 15, with eye 4, verbal 5, and motor 6.
Initial ECG is in place, and treatments are underway. We will now perform a 12-lead EKG for a more detailed cardiac assessment.
12-lead EKG is completed. The interpretation is normal for a minor and shows sinus rhythm.
Her breathing is still at 18 breaths per minute, and she is speaking in longer sentences now.
We are now going to give a second dose of albuterol via nebulizer.
Her SpO2 is at 97 with no new signs of airway distress.
Ma'am, we are going to take you to VCU Health Systems for further evaluation. Is that okay with you?
We are now going to secure you to the stretcher for transport.
She is secured with a 5-point harness. All gear and medications are in place. We are ready.
I'll call the hospital now and update them on your condition and treatments so far.
The hospital staff will be expecting an 11-year-old patient with asthma exacerbation. She has been given two doses of albuterol and 10 mg dexamethasone via IV with improving vitals.
Thank you both for helping my granddaughter. I really appreciate it.
Thank you as well. We are glad we could help.
Alright, let's prepare the stretcher for transport. All set?
Aye-aye, all set. Let's begin.
Hospital, we're en route with an 11-year-old female. Initial vitals were 48 resp, 105 bpm, and SpO2 of 75. Improved to 18 resp, 94 bpm, and 97% saturation.
IV is in, treatments administered, and 12-lead completed. Stable for transport.
Blood glucose is still 104, which is normal for her age.
Let me confirm the medications given before arrival: two rounds of albuterol and one dose of dexamethasone.
All medications and procedures are verified. Hospital, we'll be arriving in five minutes.
Double-check for all IV lines and monitor wires. All systems are secure.
Final EKG and respiration reading: 18 breaths per minute, still in sinus rhythm. Progress is encouraging.
Latest blood pressure is 135 over 76, trending stable.
We are following the EMS protocol for asthma exacerbation due to her symptoms and medical history. No急性 signs yet.
SpO2 is now 98, a steady improvement.
Smooth transport so far. No new issues reported.
Let me check your chest again. Does the sharp sensation still persist?
It is better but still present.
Good to hear it's improving. Did you eat or drink anything prior to your symptoms starting?
I had some cereal and milk in the morning while I was watching TV.
No signs of rashes, bites, or other skin issues. Skin remains normal in appearance.
Hospital, we're at the ED entrance with the 11-year-old patient. Please assist in the unloading process.
Transfer to the ED will be handled smoothly. IV and monitoring systems remain stable.
Handing off care to the ED nursing staff with the full report and medications documented. Everything in order.
All set and ready for handover. Great job on this call.
We'll wish you a full recovery, Kayla. If you need anything else, don't hesitate to reach out.
Affirmed. Next call, we'll see each other out there.
